checks.a_analysis: The output text strictly follows the source document's content for all provided sections. No fabricated sentences or paragraphs were identified. AI-generated metadata titles and image alt texts are within acceptable limits as defined in the instructions.

The provided JSON sections do not contain any URLs, email addresses, or phone numbers. All mentioned entities (like NC.gov) appear in the context of the source text. No new or incorrect contact information was added.

All pages of the source document are accounted for. Pages 1-3, 19-20, and 27-28 are present in the JSON. The remaining pages (4-18 and 21-26) are correctly represented by a placeholder indicating they were omitted for token budget, which matches the count of missing pages (21).
Tables representing sidebar data on pages 19 and 20 are present and contain the correct number of rows relative to the information shown in the source PDF.

checks.check_name: Significant text omission within a section
checks.a_analysis: In Section 1, which corresponds to Page 2 ('Inside This Report'), all text labels for the table of contents items are missing from the text representation. While the items are represented as images, the text strings (e.g., 'About NCDIT', 'Financial Information', 'Closing the Digital Divide') are not transcribed as text elements. This constitutes a near-total omission of the textual data for this page beyond the main heading, and the information is not captured in image alt text either.
checks.b_evidence_source: Text labels on Page 2: 'From the Secretary', 'About NCDIT', 'Enterprise Functions and Divisions', 'Boards and Commissions', 'Financial Information', 'Priorities and Goals', 'Goal Highlights', 'Closing the Digital Divide', 'Creating COVIDSafeNC', 'NCDIT Goes Virtual with Webinar Series', 'Awards and Recognition', 'Looking Ahead'.
checks.b_evidence_output: Section 1 contains only the heading 'Inside This Report' and a series of image elements with alt text that does not include the item labels.

checks.check_name: Minor text differences beyond acceptable reformatting
checks.a_analysis: The text in the output accurately reflects the source. Minor corrections (such as fixing 'do not know have' to 'do not have' on page 19) are considered acceptable normalization that does not negatively impact information fidelity. Information in tables matches the sidebars in the source.

checks.a_analysis: I examined the output for any instances of duplicated text blocks or sections. The text regarding the '$1 billion from the infrastructure package' on page 27 appears twice in the source (once in the body text and once in a callout box); the JSON output correctly represents the body text as a paragraph (reading_order 336) and the callout box as an image with alt text (reading_order 343), avoiding text duplication.

The heading hierarchy in the output correctly identifies major section titles as H1 and sub-sections as H2 or H3. however, there is an error in the nesting of sibling headings. On page 19, 'GREAT Grant Program' and 'Fiber Access to Rural Communities' are sibling sub-sections under the H1 'Closing the Digital Divide' and are correctly marked as H2. On page 20, 'Satellite Internet Technologies for Student Connectivity Pilot' is also a sibling sub-section, but it is marked as H3 (reading_order 230), incorrectly nesting it as a child of the preceding H2.
checks.b_evidence_source: Page 20 contains the heading 'Satellite Internet Technologies for Student Connectivity Pilot', which is stylistically a sibling to the headings on page 19.
checks.b_evidence_output: The heading 'Satellite Internet Technologies for Student Connectivity Pilot' is assigned level 3 (reading_order 230), whereas its sibling heading 'Fiber Access to Rural Communities' is assigned level 2 (reading_order 225).

checks.a_analysis: The sidebars on pages 19 and 20, which present statistics and descriptions, have been converted into tables in the JSON output (reading_order 227 and 244). These tables have misidentified headers. In both instances, only the first statistic (e.g., '1M+') is marked as a header cell, while subsequent statistics (e.g., '$30M+', '$26M+') are placed in data cells. This inconsistent identification corrupts the structural organization of the data, as all statistics in these sidebars serve the same role as 'keys' or headers for their respective descriptions.
checks.b_evidence_source: Sidebars on page 19 and 20 list statistics ('1M+', '$30M+', '$26M+') followed by their descriptions.
checks.b_evidence_output: In the table at reading_order 227, '1M+' is a header cell, but '$30M+' and '$26M+' are data cells within the body of the table. The same occurs in the table at reading_order 244 for statistics '11', '32', and '14'.

checks.a_analysis: The reading order of the document follows a logical top-to-bottom, left-to-right flow that is consistent with the source document. Sidebars are moved to follow the main text of the section they appear in, which is an acceptable adjustment for accessibility and structural flow.

The lists found in the document, such as the goals and priorities on pages 5 and 13 (though some are in omitted sections), are generally represented well. The sidebar statistics on pages 19-20 were converted to tables rather than lists, but this is handled under the table check (S3). No major list nesting or merging errors were identified in the visible portions of the JSON.

I verified several URLs present in the source document (such as the YouTube and StateScoop links on page 24). These links are correctly captured in the document (although some fall into omitted sections of the provided JSON snippet). I found no evidence of fabricated or broken links in the visible sections.

checks.a_analysis: The HTML output correctly maintains the visual organization and flow of the original multi-page PDF document. Sections are presented in the same order as the source, and the transition from a multi-column, multi-page layout to a single-column, responsive HTML layout is logical and does not confuse the meaning or flow of information.

The HTML uses table structures to represent the callout statistics and data boxes found on pages 19 and 20 of the source PDF. These are rendered clearly and are easily readable, maintaining the relationship between the headers (e.g., '1M+', '40,000') and their corresponding descriptive text. Sighted users can easily understand the data.

While most meaningful photos and graphics are present in the HTML output, the two major pie charts from the 'Financial Information' section are missing. Specifically, the pie chart on page 11 illustrating budget expenditures and the pie chart on page 12 illustrating funding sources are absent from the rendered HTML. These are significant data visualizations and their omission constitutes a major defect. Other graphics, such as the cover art and various illustrations, are present.
checks.b_evidence_source: Pages 11 and 12 of the source PDF contain large multi-colored pie charts representing 'Budget Expenditures as of June 30, 2021' and 'Budget Funding Sources as of June 30, 2021'.
checks.b_evidence_output: In the 'Financial Information' section of the HTML output, the text-based legends for these charts are present, but the primary pie chart images themselves are missing.
checks.b_evidence_location: Financial Information section, corresponding to source pages 11 and 12.

checks.a_analysis: There is no evidence of visual duplication of body content (paragraphs or tables) in the HTML output. Content that appears twice in the PDF (such as the Secretary's title as both a sign-off and a photo caption) is correctly reflected in the HTML without additional unintended duplication.
